steps.instruction: A scatterplot of head length vs. total body length for 104 possums is loaded. Before looking at any statistics, describe the **direction** of the association.
steps.observe: As total body length increases, does head length tend to increase, decrease, or stay the same? This tells you whether the association is positive, negative, or nonexistent.
steps.reveal: The association is **positive** — possums with longer bodies tend to have longer heads. The points generally rise from left to right.

steps.instruction: Now describe the **form** of the association. Does the cloud of points follow a straight-line pattern, or does it curve?
steps.observe: Imagine drawing a line through the middle of the point cloud. Would a straight line capture the trend well, or would you need a curve? Look for any systematic bending.
steps.reveal: The relationship is approximately **linear** — a straight line captures the general trend reasonably well. There is no obvious curve or bend in the pattern.

steps.instruction: How **strong** is the association? Think about how tightly the points cluster around the trend.
steps.observe: If you drew a line through the middle, how close would most points be to it? Are the points tightly packed (strong) or widely scattered (weak)?
steps.reveal: The association is **moderate** — the points follow the upward trend, but there is noticeable scatter. For any given body length, head lengths vary by several millimeters. A strong association would show points hugging the line much more tightly.

steps.instruction: Toggle on the **regression line** (if available). Look for any points that fall far from the line — these are potential **outliers** or **influential points**.
steps.observe: Are there any possums whose head length is much larger or smaller than you would predict from their body length? Are there any possums with unusually short or long bodies compared to the rest?
steps.reveal: When describing a scatterplot, always check for unusual observations. Points far from the trend (vertically) may indicate an unusual individual. Points far from the rest horizontally (high **leverage**) can disproportionately influence the regression line. Reporting outliers is the fourth step in describing an association: **direction**, **form**, **strength**, and **unusual observations**.

steps.instruction: Try selecting different pairs of variables from the dataset using the variable selectors. Find a pair that shows a **negative** association, or a pair that shows **no** association.
steps.observe: Not all pairs of variables are related. When there is no association, the scatterplot looks like a shapeless cloud with no trend.
steps.reveal: Exploring multiple variable pairs reinforces that association is not guaranteed — you need to actually look at the data. Some pairs will show strong patterns, others will show nothing. The scatterplot is your first and most important tool for exploring the relationship between two quantitative variables.
